pi_items.verified_value: GCompris is real, publicly-available open-source educational software maintained by KDE (GNU AGPL v3 licensed). Current version is 26.0 (released February 4, 2026) with 197 activities. Version 2.3 (installed in this environment) has 180+ activities.
pi_items.source: Web searches: gcompris.net, github.com/gcompris, KDE official sources; install_gcompris.sh confirms installation of gcompris-qt package

pi_items.metadata_value: Wolf, Goat, and Cabbage logic activity in GCompris Strategy or Logic category
pi_items.verified_value: NO EVIDENCE FOUND. Despite extensive searches of GCompris official documentation, activities lists, GitHub repository, and news/changelog entries, no 'Wolf, Goat, and Cabbage' activity exists in GCompris version 2.3 or any other documented version (4.0-26.0). Confirmed GCompris activities in logic/puzzle categories include: Chess, Checkers, Sudoku, Hanoi, Maze, Tangram, Fifteen, Traffic, Graph-coloring, Lightsoff, Hexagon, and others. The classic river-crossing puzzle is well-known educational content, but is NOT an activity in GCompris.
pi_items.source: Web searches: gcompris.net screenshots, KDE Activities documentation, GitHub repository file listing, official release notes and changelogs; multiple queries for 'wolf goat cabbage gcompris', 'gcompris river crossing', complete activities lists - all returned no evidence of this activity existing

pi_items.metadata_value: Open 'Wolf, Goat, and Cabbage' activity in GCompris Strategy or Logic category
pi_items.verified_value: Task description accurately describes what SHOULD happen (navigate to activity, solve river crossing puzzle, capture screenshots), but the activity referenced does NOT exist in GCompris. The task appears to assume an activity that was either never implemented or is planned/hypothetical.
pi_items.source: Web searches confirming activity does not exist; task description in task.json lines 5

pi_items.metadata_value: Wolf eats Goat, Goat eats Cabbage if left alone
pi_items.verified_value: The puzzle constraints described are historically accurate. The wolf-goat-cabbage river crossing puzzle originates from at least the 9th century (Alcuin of York) and the constraint rules are correct: Wolf cannot be alone with Goat, Goat cannot be alone with Cabbage. The intermediate state (Goat on right, others on left, boat returned to left) is a valid step in the canonical solution.
pi_items.source: Web searches: Wikipedia, GeeksforGeeks, MathIsFun confirm puzzle rules and solution sequence

privileged_info_summary: CRITICAL FINDING: The 'Wolf, Goat, and Cabbage' activity referenced in this task does NOT exist in GCompris version 2.3 (installed) or any documented GCompris version. Extensive searches of official GCompris documentation, GitHub repository, release notes, and activity listings found no evidence of this activity. The classic wolf-goat-cabbage river-crossing puzzle is a real, well-known logic puzzle (dating to at least the 9th century), but it is not an activity in GCompris. All other verified facts: GCompris is real software (open-source, KDE-maintained, version 2.3 with 180+ activities installed); file paths /home/ga/Documents/river_*.png are syntactically correct and used consistently; scoring breakdown (20+30+30+20=100 points, 80 to pass) exactly matches verifier.py implementation. The task would be impossible to complete as specified because the required GCompris activity does not exist.
